text: a combined robotic approach of a synchronous upper rectal and prostate cancer. The patient is a fifty-six-year-old male with no medical surgical history of interest diagnosed with an adenocarcinoma of the rectum at ten centimeters from the anal verge in combination with a prostate adenocarcinoma. These are the MRI images. Preoperatively, the patient received neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy for rectal cancer. This slide shows trochour placement. We use the same trocar combination for both procedures as it allowed us to reach both surgical fields without technical difficulties. For this procedure, we use the da Vinci's eye surgical system. The patient car was placed in pelvic position.

After carefully surgical team consideration, we decided to start the surgery approaching the rectum first for the three reasons showed in this slide, which will be discussed during the video. Once completed both procedures, specimens were extracted through a menial operotomy and a loop ileostomy constructed.

The surgeon performs dissection and ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery using hemo-lock clips, followed by the sectioning of the inferior mesenteric vein with bipolar sealing. The colon is released from its left parietal colic attachments, and a mesorectal excision is achieved following the holy plane. The splenic flexure is not taken down in this patient.

The surgeon uses an endostapler to section the rectum. The rectal specimen is extracted through a mini laparotomy, and the distal colon is prepared for a tension-free colorectal anastomosis. An end-to-end stapling is performed, followed by verification of the anastomosis viability using air insufflation through the rectum, a leak test, and an endocyanin green vascularization test.

The surgeon initiates radical prostatectomy by accessing the Retzius space, dissecting the endopelvic fascia and prostatovesical junction. An incision is made in the anterior neck of the bladder, exposing the Foley catheter. The right vas deferens is dissected and sectioned, followed by the exposure of the right seminal vesicle. Lateral dissection of vascular pedicles is performed with careful preservation of neurovascular bundles. The same procedure is repeated on the left side.
The surgeon releases the urethral cuff and sections the prostate specimen, followed by its extraction. The urethral anastomosis is then performed, and a leakage test is conducted to ensure integrity.
The surgeon constructs a loop ileostomy at the site of the assistant trocar port, showing the final result of the procedure.
The patient was discharged on the sixth postoperative day after a favorable clinical course with no complications. Thanks for your attention.
